narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, including your plan to attend full-time like most students and a middle-class family income background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 59.7% chance that you would fail it, tipping the scales just over the threshold for rejection. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your academic record that carried the most weight in the decision. The strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, far below the average decile of about 5.5. Nearly balancing that out was your strong undergraduate GPA, which pulled strongly in the opposite direction, signaling potential for success and performing better than most applicants. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also nudged the prediction toward failure, again showing early struggles relative to the typical mid-range performance around the 5.7 average. Compared to other students, your LSAT score of 28 is notably lower than the 36.7 average, though it played a smaller role here. While your background as a Black male brings unique strengths and perspectives to law, the model focused heavily on these grade deciles as the top predictors of bar outcomes. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience—many overcome early hurdles—but the system's goal is to flag patterns linked to first-time bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, seeking targeted academic support to improve your decile rankings in future terms, or exploring programs with bar passage support tailored for diverse students like yourself. Building a stronger narrative around your upward trajectory could also help in reapplying or appealing to schools with holistic reviews.
